title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
text:
India’s Parliament has two chambers with nearly identical legislative powers but very different electoral mandates. One is elected directly from 543 constituencies. The other represents states. Running the same NLP pipeline on both produces a clean natural experiment: what happens to parliamentary language when you remove the constituency?


Piyush Zaware



4,315 LS Questions


9,315 RS Questions


860 LS MPs · 264 RS Members


SVD · TF-IDF · BING Sentiment


2014 – 2026
Lok Sabha starred questions come from MPs who won individual seats. The question they ask this session reflects their constituency’s needs as much as their party’s ideology. A BJP MP from coastal Andhra will ask about fisheries. A Congress MP from Chhattisgarh will ask about forest rights. Party affiliation is one signal among several.
Rajya Sabha members are elected by state legislatures, not voters. They have no individual constituency to service. If the constituency pull is the main reason Lok Sabha parties don’t cluster by ideology in their questioning vocabulary, Rajya Sabha members should show clearer party-line vocabulary differences. That is the hypothesis this comparison tests.

title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
text:
Coverage and Scale

  4,315LS starred questions
  9,315RS starred questions
  860LS MPs matched
  264RS members matched

Lok Sabha generates roughly 22% more starred questions in aggregate, despite the two houses being closer in formal power than that ratio suggests. The gap reflects how the instruments function in practice: starred questions in Lok Sabha are partly constituency signalling tools, so there is structural incentive to ask more of them. RS members face no such incentive from voters.

title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
section: The Volume Question: Who Drives Each House?
text:
The Volume Question: Who Drives Each House?



Starred questions per matched member, by party and house (2014-2025). The metric normalises for both party size and house size, isolating question-asking intensity at the individual member level.


The raw question count misleads. BJP held 282-303 seats across the 16th and 17th Lok Sabhas, which mechanically produces a large absolute volume. And Rajya Sabha has 245 seats to Lok Sabha’s 543, which compresses RS totals before any party-level comparison. The per-member rate strips both of those confounds.
The result is striking: across every single party, RS members ask dramatically more questions per person than their LS counterparts – roughly 5 to 10 times more. This is not a BJP-specific finding; it holds for INC, Left, TMC, SP, and every other party in the data.
The explanation is structural. Lok Sabha starred questions are distributed by lottery across a house of 543 members. When BJP holds 300 of those seats, each INC MP gets fewer draws. In Rajya Sabha, the seat distribution was more balanced throughout the Modi era – BJP typically held 35-40% of seats, not 55-56% – so opposition members had proportionally more floor access.






NoteParty-specific patterns worth noting



BJP is the only party whose RS rate (around 18 questions per member) is lower than that of most opposition parties in RS. Governing party backbenchers have limited incentive to use starred questions to hold their own government accountable. That BJP RS members ask 18 questions per member at all reflects that RS was not under BJP majority for much of this period.
DMK has a near-zero LS bar (essentially no matched LS questions) but 34 questions per RS member. DMK had very few Lok Sabha seats in the 16th and 17th parliaments – they were locked out at the national level. Rajya Sabha, filled via Tamil Nadu’s state assembly, was their only consistent national platform through those years.
JDU and AAP follow the same pattern. Their LS presence is too thin to register, but their RS members are highly active, using the upper house as a national megaphone that constituency elections had not yet granted them.
Regional parties – TDP, SP, DMK – cluster at the top in RS rates, above 30 questions per member. These are small national parties with focused issue agendas and no obligation to distribute their questions across hundreds of diverse constituencies. They concentrate their RS access intensely.

title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
section: What Words Separate the Two Houses?
text:
What Words Separate the Two Houses?
The simplest question: are the vocabularies of the two houses actually different? Take all question titles from both houses, compute word frequencies, and ask which words appear disproportionately in one versus the other.



Log2 ratio of word frequency in RS vs LS question titles. Orange bars point rightward = that word appears proportionally more in Rajya Sabha. Blue bars point leftward = that word is more characteristic of Lok Sabha.








NoteWhat the vocabulary split reveals



Rajya Sabha distinctive words trend toward the abstract and constitutional: policy, rights, bill, national, regulation, agreement, amendment, trade. These are the words of deliberative policy debate. A Rajya Sabha member asking about trade agreements or proposed legislation is operating in the register appropriate to a chamber that reviews laws rather than delivering constituency services.
Lok Sabha distinctive words trend toward the concrete and local: pradesh, districts, state, village, construction, pending, demand, implementation. These are the words of constituency oversight. A Lok Sabha MP asking why the construction of a state highway is pending in their district is using parliament as a direct accountability instrument for local delivery.
This vocabulary split is the clearest empirical signal of the two houses operating differently. The constitutional difference (constituency vs state representation) shows up directly in the language MPs choose.



How vocabulary shifts within parties
The overall split hides interesting variation across parties. The same party’s language changes depending on which house its members are sitting in.



For BJP, INC, Left, and TMC: words used proportionally more in RS questions vs LS questions. Orange = more RS; blue = more LS. Reveals how each party’s questioning strategy changes across chambers.








NoteParty-specific shifts



BJP shifts from infrastructure and scheme-specific vocabulary in Lok Sabha (where its large MP contingent asks about roads, funds, pradhan mantri schemes at the constituency level) toward more national policy vocabulary in Rajya Sabha.
INC shows a smaller shift, consistent with its opposition role in both houses. In Lok Sabha, INC MPs concentrate on accountability questions about specific government actions. In Rajya Sabha, INC language tilts toward constitutional and legislative vocabulary, where the upper house is the better venue for challenging government bills.
Left shows the most pronounced shift. In Lok Sabha, Left MPs concentrated heavily on Kerala and West Bengal constituency concerns. In Rajya Sabha, where they have no local constituency, their vocabulary converges on labour rights and national policy terms – the ideological core they fall back on when constituency pull disappears.
TMC in Lok Sabha is almost entirely West Bengal-focused. In Rajya Sabha, the constituency anchor is loosened, and TMC members broaden into national welfare and rights vocabulary.

title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
section: Ministry Targeting: Same Party, Same Ministries?
text:
Ministry Targeting: Same Party, Same Ministries?
Parties have consistent strategic priorities around which ministries to scrutinise. But do those priorities survive the shift from a constituency-based house to a state-based one?



Ministry targeting by party in Lok Sabha (left) vs Rajya Sabha (right). Cell colour = % of that party’s questions directed to each ministry.








NoteThe ministries that shift most between houses



Finance is more heavily targeted in Rajya Sabha across all major parties. The upper house is the natural venue for scrutinising fiscal policy – it has no constituency-specific infrastructure spending to track, so questions gravitate toward national macroeconomic and banking policy.
Railways and Road Transport dominate Lok Sabha targeting. These are the two ministries most directly tied to constituency-level infrastructure spending. An MP asking why a road project is delayed or when a new railway station will open is doing classic constituency work that has no analog in Rajya Sabha.
Home Affairs and Labour show a more symmetric pattern: both houses scrutinise these ministries at roughly the same rate, with opposition parties concentrating there regardless of chamber. This makes sense – internal security and employment are not constituency-specific concerns; they are national policy debates.
The pattern supports a clean institutional interpretation: parties use Lok Sabha for constituency accountability, Rajya Sabha for national policy scrutiny. The party-level priorities (which party focuses on which ministry) are consistent across houses, but the type of question asked changes.



Rajya Sabha ministry excess targeting



Pearson residuals for RS ministry targeting. Red = party over-questions this ministry relative to its overall question share; blue = under-questions. The pattern broadly mirrors Lok Sabha but with finance and home affairs more pronounced.

title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
section: Party Discipline: Does the Upper House Coordinate Better?
text:
Party Discipline: Does the Upper House Coordinate Better?
A disciplined parliamentary party’s MPs ask about the same topics using similar vocabulary. The discipline score is the average cosine similarity between each member’s TF-IDF vector and the party’s centroid. Higher = more coordinated questioning.
The constituency hypothesis predicts RS members should be more disciplined than LS MPs: without individual constituency agendas pulling them in different directions, they should converge on a shared party vocabulary.



Discipline scores by party in both houses. Connected dots show the same party in LS (blue) and RS (orange). A dot moving rightward when going from LS to RS means RS members are more coordinated.








NoteThe discipline finding: modest convergence, not transformation



BJP RS discipline (0.185) vs LS (0.105): RS members are 76% more disciplined. BJP sends far fewer members to Rajya Sabha than to Lok Sabha, and without the constituency heterogeneity of 68 members from very different states, the centroid is easier to converge on.
INC RS discipline (0.201) vs LS (0.166): The INC pattern mirrors BJP. Fewer RS members, more coherent opposition agenda, slightly higher discipline.
The general pattern: nearly all parties show higher discipline in Rajya Sabha, consistent with the constituency pull hypothesis. But the magnitude of the shift is modest. Discipline roughly doubles from the lowest-discipline parties, but it does not reach the level of true caucus coordination one would expect in a whipped chamber. Indian parties, in both houses, give their members substantial latitude on what to ask about.
Smaller parties (Left, DMK, BJD, JDU) score high on discipline in both houses. Their members are already highly focused on a narrow issue set regardless of chamber, so the constituency removal makes little additional difference.

title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
section: Adversarialism: Which House is More Confrontational?
text:
Adversarialism: Which House is More Confrontational?
Opposition parties use starred questions as accountability tools. But does the nature of the chamber affect how adversarial the questioning gets? Lok Sabha MPs face re-election and may moderate aggressive questioning to maintain local relationships. Rajya Sabha members, insulated from direct voter accountability, might be freer to confront.
Sentiment is measured using the BING positive/negative lexicon applied to question titles. A question is classified as adversarial if its title contains more negative than positive words. The measure captures adversarial framing (shortage, failure, delay, violation) vs aspirational framing (promotion, welfare, development, scheme).



Adversarialism rate (% of questions with net-negative BING sentiment) by party in both houses. Connected dots show LS (blue) vs RS (orange).








NoteWhat the adversarialism comparison shows



Left parties are the most adversarial in both houses, with 70.1% of their RS question titles scoring net-negative in BING sentiment. This is consistent across chambers: left parties adopt an adversarial stance as a matter of strategic identity.
The RS-vs-LS gap is small. Most parties show similar adversarialism rates in both houses. This suggests that the tone of parliamentary questioning is a party-level strategic choice, not a function of how insulated the member is from voters. A party that frames questions adversarially in Lok Sabha does the same in Rajya Sabha.
The exception is regional parties (DMK, AIADMK, BJD), which show somewhat higher adversarialism in RS than LS. In LS, these parties balance adversarial questions with aspirational constituency-service questions. In RS, without the aspirational constituency component, the balance tips toward confrontation.
BJP is consistently the least adversarial in both houses, which is structurally expected: government parties ask fewer critical questions about their own policies. The slight uptick in RS adversarialism for BJP may reflect RS members, with longer terms and no constituency to protect, occasionally willing to criticise implementation gaps that their LS colleagues prefer to gloss over.

title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
text:
The Constituency Hypothesis: Test Results
The main theoretical question: does removing constituency variation (by moving from LS to RS) produce more party-based vocabulary sorting in the ideal point space?
The test runs the identical truncated SVD pipeline on both houses. If constituency variation is the main reason Lok Sabha parties don’t cluster by ideology, Rajya Sabha parties should cluster more clearly.

Party clustering: between-party vs within-party variance



Between-party variance / within-party variance on the primary SVD dimension, Lok Sabha vs Rajya Sabha. A higher ratio means more party-based sorting.








NoteInterpreting the variance ratio



The between/within ratio for Lok Sabha is 0.8206. For Rajya Sabha it is 0.2255 – lower, not higher.
This is the opposite of what the constituency hypothesis predicts, and the reason is worth being precise about. LS is dominated by BJP, which accounts for roughly 52% of matched questions. BJP’s vocabulary as the governing party (infrastructure achievements, scheme rollouts, development targets) is systematically different from every opposition party’s vocabulary (failures, delays, accountability). That contrast inflates the between-party component in LS mechanically. In Rajya Sabha, where BJP held 35-40% of seats rather than 55%, the dominant-party effect is weaker, and the ratio falls.
Put differently: the LS B/W ratio captures a governing-vs-opposition vocabulary split as much as party ideology. The RS ratio is smaller because the house is closer to balance, and with no single party dominating, vocabulary variation spreads within rather than between parties.
In both houses, the absolute level is far below US Congress NOMINATE (>10). India’s parliamentary parties do not enforce coordinated question vocabularies. Members ask about what they know, where they are from, and what their committee positions require – not what a party whip mandates.




Ideal point maps: LS vs RS






Lok Sabha: 860 MPs in 2D SVD space. Party centroids (diamonds) cluster near the origin with wide within-party spread.







Rajya Sabha: 264 members in 2D SVD space. Centroids show slightly more separation, but within-party variance still dominates.







Distribution along the primary dimension






LS Dimension 1 distributions by party. Distributions heavily overlap. The primary axis is geographic/sectoral, not ideological.







RS Dimension 1 distributions by party. BJP and INC are now visibly separated, but the overlap remains substantial.





The two distribution plots make the constituency hypothesis concrete. In Lok Sabha (left), the BJP and INC distributions are nearly identical – they cover the same vocabulary space because their MPs come from similar mixes of states and constituency types. In Rajya Sabha (right), a gap opens up. BJP members lean toward one vocabulary register, INC toward another. But the gap is far narrower than what you would expect if party ideology were the primary driver of question content.

title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
text:
What This Comparison Tells Us

  5–10xmore questions per RS member than LS
  < 1.0between/within ratio in both houses
  10+NOMINATE ratio for US Congress

The comparison across houses produces three conclusions.
The vocabulary split is real. LS questions are more local in language (pradesh, districts, construction, pending); RS questions are more national (policy, bill, rights, regulation). This is exactly what the constitutional difference between the houses predicts, and it shows up cleanly in the log-ratio chart. The per-member question rate is also dramatically higher in RS – 5 to 10 times higher across every party – because RS offered a more level platform when BJP held supermajorities in LS.
Party clustering does not increase in the upper house. The between/within variance ratio is higher in Lok Sabha (B/W = 0.8206) than in Rajya Sabha (0.2255). This appears to reflect the governing-vs-opposition contrast that BJP’s LS dominance creates, rather than genuine ideological polarisation. In both houses, the ratio is far below what US NOMINATE finds for Congress (>10). Party affiliation explains a small fraction of what any individual member asks about.
Party strategy is more durable than party discipline. The ministry-level comparison shows that parties maintain consistent targeting strategies across houses – INC focuses on Finance and Home Affairs in both, Left concentrates on Labour in both, regional parties track their state-specific ministries in both. But within those strategic preferences, members have enormous latitude on exact vocabulary and framing. The parties know which ministries to scrutinise; they leave their members to figure out how.

The institutional implication
India’s two-house system does not produce two distinct types of parliamentary questioning in the way the constitutional theory predicts. Both houses show weak party coordination. Both show vocabulary driven more by individual background than collective party agenda. The upper house produces more policy-oriented language and slightly more party clustering, but not a qualitatively different mode of legislative behaviour.
The Rajya Sabha is not, in practice, a deliberative chamber of states in the way the Westminster Lords or US Senate function. It is closer to a second Lok Sabha with state-based rather than constituency-based member selection – and that difference does show up in the data, but as degree rather than kind.

title: Two Houses, One Parliament: How Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha Ask Differently
text:
Methodology Note
All analyses on this page use matched starred questions from both houses (match rates: LS 95.5%, RS 96.7%). The matching pipeline, TF-IDF construction, truncated SVD, and discipline scoring are identical across houses, making the comparison clean. Sentiment uses the BING positive/negative lexicon applied to question titles, chosen for its availability and interpretability. The between/within variance ratio is computed on the primary SVD dimension; the absolute values should not be compared directly to NOMINATE, which uses a different decomposition and different legislative instrument (roll-call votes vs question vocabulary).
The analysis compares institutions, not individuals. Claims about “BJP” or “INC” behaviour are claims about the aggregate of their members’ questions, not about any specific MP’s intent.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
I ran unsupervised machine learning on 150,000 parliamentary questions from three Lok Sabhas. No labels, no hand-coding. Just the raw text of what Indian MPs ask the government, and what falls out when you let the data speak.


Piyush Zaware



150,000 Questions


16th – 18th Lok Sabha (2014–2026)


13 Parties


LDA · STM · Word2Vec · UMAP


BERTopic · VADER · spaCy NER


Co-questioning Network · igraph

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
The Data

  150,629questions
  13parties
  205documents
  3,258vocabulary terms




The most frequent subjects in parliamentary questions across 150,000 starred and unstarred questions, 16th–18th Lok Sabha. Size proportional to question frequency after removing parliamentary boilerplate.


The dataset covers the 16th (2014-2019), 17th (2019-2024), and 18th (2024-) Lok Sabha. I focused on starred questions since they carry more political content. Unstarred questions are written submissions; starred ones require a minister to answer in the House, so parties pick them more carefully.
Matching MPs to their parties requires handling a consistent set of problems in the raw records: honorific prefixes in varying formats (Dr., Prof., Adv.), word-order reversals across data sources, compound initials, and spelling variation. A multi-pass normalization pipeline resolves these, achieving a 95.5% match rate on starred questions across all major parties and all three Lok Sabhas. The remaining 4.5% are Tamil Nadu AIADMK MPs from the 16th Lok Sabha whose names were absent from the Wikipedia constituency lists used as the lookup source.
The unit of analysis is the party-session document: all starred questions submitted by a party in a given session, concatenated into one text. That gives 205 documents and a 3,258-term vocabulary after pruning.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Methods








Method
What it does
Output




LDA
Finds recurring topics across all documents without supervision. Each document is a mixture; each topic is a set of words.
15 topics, party topic weights


STM
Same as LDA but lets party identity and time shift topic prevalence. Asks whether BJP talks more about topic X than INC, and whether that changed.
Marginal party and time effects per topic


Word2Vec + Kozlowski (2019)
Trains word embeddings on 150K questions. Ideological dimensions are directions in this space, defined by antonym pairs.
Party positions on Hindutva, Left-Right, Rural-Urban


HAC
Hierarchical clustering of parties by vocabulary distance. No preset number of clusters.
Dendrogram


GMM
Soft clustering of party-session documents in PCA space.
6-cluster solution


UMAP / t-SNE
Dimensionality reduction from 3,258 dimensions to 2D.
Visual maps of the corpus









Note



On the word embedding approach. The idea from Kozlowski, Taddy & Evans (2019) is that you can encode an ideological dimension as a direction in word embedding space. The Hindutva dimension, for example, runs from words like {secular, minority, mosque, constitution} toward words like {hindu, mandir, temple, cow, ram, ayodhya}. A party’s position on this axis is just the average projection of its vocabulary onto that direction, weighted by TF-IDF. No human labelling involved.
Source: “The Geometry of Culture”, American Sociological Review, 2019.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Results

1. The Ideological Map
Start with the simplest question: does the word embedding space recover anything that looks like Indian political ideology?



Party vocabulary positions in two-dimensional ideological space. Economic axis (x): pro-labour/welfare to market/growth. Cultural axis (y): secular to Hindu nationalist. Each point is a party’s mean projection across all sessions.





Raw output: party positions on two ideological dimensions recovered by word2vec + antonym projection. X-axis = market/growth (right) vs welfare/labour (left). Y-axis = secular (bottom) vs Hindu nationalist (top).


It does. BJP and Shiv Sena are at the nationalist end. Left parties and AAP are at the secular end. INC is in the centre-left. The ordering you’d expect if you just asked a political scientist to draw it by hand is basically what comes out.
The economic axis has some surprises. BSP and RJD use the most redistributive, pro-poor language in Parliament. TDP, despite being a regional party, skews strongly toward market and investment vocabulary. AAP is the interesting case: it sits secular but economically rightward, which fits its governance-and-anti-corruption brand but puts it in an odd place relative to the traditional left.

Positive Hindutva = more nationalist vocabulary. Positive Economic Left = more labour/welfare vocabulary. Positive Rural = more agrarian vocabulary.


2. BJP’s Language Has Shifted
BJP’s score on the Hindutva dimension goes up with each Lok Sabha.



BJP’s mean Hindutva score per Lok Sabha. Higher = more religious and nationalist vocabulary in parliamentary questions.


It is not a dramatic jump. But it is monotonic across 16th, 17th, and 18th, which covers a decade in government. The vocabulary BJP MPs reach for when they question ministries has gradually shifted toward religious and civilisational terms. Whether you read that as ideological deepening or just a party mirroring its base is a separate question. The shift is there in the text either way.


3. What Parliament Actually Talks About
LDA finds 15 recurring topics. The curve below shows how model fit improves as you add more topics; 15 was chosen at the point where adding more stops helping much.



Held-out perplexity by number of topics. Lower = better fit. K=15 at the elbow.


The topics that emerge are coherent and recognisable.



Top terms per topic across four ranking methods. Each column is one topic.


You get railway and highway infrastructure, farmer welfare and MSP, sugar industry regulation, judicial and electoral oversight, public health and AIIMS, higher education, water and river projects, petroleum pricing, border and defence, and state-level budget allocations. These are not surprising. They are the recurring preoccupations of Indian parliamentary life.
What is interesting is how unevenly they are distributed across parties.



LDA topic heatmap. Each row is a topic, each column is a party. Brighter = that party asks more questions on that topic relative to its overall volume.


BJP dominates infrastructure and defence topics. INC clusters around governance, accountability, and judicial matters, which makes sense for a party in opposition trying to question process rather than specific policies. Left parties are concentrated almost entirely on labour, wages, and public sector employment. Regional parties light up on narrow, state-specific topics: NCP on Maharashtra agriculture, DMK on water disputes and Dravidian cultural issues.


4. Party Effects and Time Trends
STM goes further than LDA by estimating how party identity and time shift topic prevalence directly.






Topics growing or shrinking across the three Lok Sabhas. Red = statistically significant shift (p < 0.05). Positive = topic is asked about more over time.







BJP vs INC topic contrast. Each point is one topic. Upper left = BJP-specific topics. Lower right = INC-specific topics.





The time trend shows which parliamentary concerns have grown and which have faded across Lok Sabhas. Some infrastructure topics expand over time; some governance topics contract. The BJP-INC contrast separates them cleanly on the dimensions you would expect: BJP on infrastructure and development, INC on accountability and oversight.


5. Mapping the Corpus
All 205 party-session documents projected from the 3,258-term TF-IDF space down to two dimensions. UMAP on the left preserves global structure; t-SNE on the right sharpens local clusters.






UMAP. Each point is one party in one session. Colour = party.







t-SNE of the same 205 documents.





Both methods put BJP and INC at the centre as the two largest clouds. Left parties form a tight knot, which is what you would expect from parties that have a fairly consistent ideological line on what to ask about. Regional parties scatter outward, pulled by their state-specific vocabularies. The two projections agree on the broad picture but differ in how they treat the smaller parties: t-SNE stretches them further apart.


6. Which Parties Sound Like Each Other
Two clustering approaches on the same TF-IDF vectors: HAC builds a tree without requiring a preset number of groups, GMM fits soft clusters in PCA space.






Hierarchical dendrogram. Parties that merge at low heights have similar vocabulary.







GMM clustering in PCA space. Six clusters, BIC-selected.





The dendrogram groups BJP with JDU and TDP, which both governed alongside BJP and reflect shared infrastructure priorities in their questions. INC, SP, and BSP cluster together with a shared welfare-oriented vocabulary. Left parties form their own tight sub-cluster. Regional parties (TMC, DMK) branch off separately.



Party positions in PCA space with GMM cluster colouring.




7. Which Ministries Get Questioned
Questions are addressed to specific ministries. This shows where each party directs its scrutiny.



Share of questions directed at each ministry, by party.


BJP MPs question Railways and Road Transport heavily. INC focuses on Finance, Home Affairs, and External Affairs. Left parties concentrate on Labour and Employment almost exclusively relative to their size. Regional parties mirror their states: DMK on Textiles and Fisheries, TDP on Agriculture, NCP on Rural Development.


8. Topic Evolution Over Time
How have the topics each party raises shifted across sessions?






BJP topic composition by session, 16th and 17th Lok Sabha. Each band is one topic.







INC topic composition across the same period. INC was in opposition throughout.





BJP’s mix shifts between the 16th and 17th Lok Sabha, with infrastructure and agriculture expanding. INC’s mix is more stable but tilts gradually toward governance and accountability topics. An opposition party in India has limited levers; questioning government process on Finance and Home Affairs is one of them.


9. What Words Live Near Each Other
The embedding lets you ask: what words appear in the most similar contexts to a given term? This is a rough map of how concepts cluster in parliamentary language.



Nearest neighbours for ten political seed words. Each panel shows the eight closest by cosine similarity.


A few things stand out. “Farmer” sits near drought, MSP, and irrigation. It is a scarcity and welfare word in Parliament, not an entrepreneurial one. “Terrorism” clusters with border, security, and Pakistan, which says something about how the term gets deployed in questions versus how it is discussed in civil society. “Caste” is proximate to reservation, OBC, and scheduled: institutional usage rather than sociological. “Corruption” lands near scam, irregularity, and vigilance. MPs raise it in procedural terms.


10. Summary



Ideological space, document map, BJP trajectory, and topic evolution in one panel.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
section: BERTopic: Topics Without the Bag-of-Words Assumption
text:
BERTopic: Topics Without the Bag-of-Words Assumption
LDA treats each document as a bag of tokens. It doesn’t know that “railway” and “train” are related, or that “farmer suicide” is a compound concept. BERTopic uses sentence embeddings instead: each question is projected into a 384-dimensional semantic space using a multilingual transformer, then HDBSCAN finds clusters of semantically similar questions, and c-TF-IDF extracts the distinctive terms per cluster.
Running BERTopic on all 11,881 starred questions (not the party-session aggregates) finds 29 coherent topics. About 25% of questions land in the outlier cluster, questions with no clean fit to any topic. That’s not a failure; it’s honest. LDA forces every document into every topic with some weight; BERTopic refuses to.



BERTopic top terms per topic, ranked by c-TF-IDF score. Each panel is one topic cluster. Terms are distinctive to the cluster, not just frequent overall, so parliamentary boilerplate drops out.


The topics that emerge are sharper than LDA’s. Railway infrastructure, power and energy, MGNREGS and rural employment, pharmaceutical drugs, banking and loans, forest and pollution, sugar mills, coastal fisheries, AIIMS and hospitals. Each topic has a clean semantic core. The LDA equivalent mixes these because the token “wise” and “proposed” appear everywhere and pollute topic boundaries.



Method comparison: LDA vs BERTopic on the same corpus.


The two methods agree on the broad thematic structure of parliamentary questioning but disagree in resolution. LDA gives 15 coarse topics with noisy term lists. BERTopic gives 29 finer topics with cleaner terms, at the cost of a 25% outlier rate and higher computational time.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Ministry Targeting
Every starred question is addressed to a specific ministry. That’s a second signal on top of the text: not just what parties talk about, but who they hold accountable. The analysis here asks whether parties target ministries strategically, or whether the distribution of questions across ministries roughly mirrors each party’s overall question volume.
The baseline is proportional questioning: if BJP asks 40% of all questions and Defence gets 5% of all questions, then BJP asking 2% of its questions to Defence is expected. The Pearson residual measures the deviation from that null.



Excess ministry targeting. Each cell shows the Pearson residual (observed minus expected, scaled by sqrt of expected). Red = over-questions; blue = under-questions. The pattern reveals legislative strategy.


A few things stand out. INC concentrates disproportionately on Finance and Home Affairs, the two ministries most exposed to accountability questions about economic policy and internal security. Left parties over-question Labour and Employment by a substantial margin. Regional parties target niche ministries tied to their state economies: Textiles, Fisheries, Steel.

Ministry concentration
Some parties spread their questions across many ministries. Others concentrate fire. The Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (sum of squared ministry shares) measures this; higher values mean more concentrated.



Ministry concentration by party (Herfindahl index). Label shows each party’s top ministry and the share of its questions directed there.


DMK and the Left are the most concentrated, both with narrow, consistent mandates. BJP and Regional are the most diffuse. BJP has a broad governing agenda; regional parties have heterogeneous state-specific priorities.


How targeting has shifted over time



BJP vs INC ministry targeting, 16th to 18th Lok Sabha. Each panel is one ministry. Lines show the share of each party’s starred questions directed there per Lok Sabha.


BJP’s targeting of Defence and Home Affairs grows across Lok Sabhas, consistent with an expanding security and sovereignty agenda. INC’s concentration on Finance increases in the 17th and 18th LS. As the opposition, it tracks economic policy failures more intensively when the government’s fiscal record becomes more contestable.


The targeting network



Bipartite party-ministry network. Edges connect parties to ministries they over-question (Pearson residual > 1.5). Edge thickness proportional to excess targeting.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Tone and Adversarialism
Parliamentary questions have a formal grammar: “Will the Minister of X please state whether…” That structure is mandatory. But within it, the choice of framing differs. Has the government failed to protect minorities? is adversarial. What schemes has the government launched to promote farmer welfare? is aspirational. VADER extracts this signal by scoring the compound sentiment of each question’s text.
The overall distribution is positive. Parliamentary questions are written to get answers, not to insult. But the relative differences across parties are informative.



Share of adversarial vs aspirational questions by party. Adversarial = VADER compound ≤ −0.05. Parties ordered by adversarialism rate.


Left parties are the most adversarial, with 25% of their starred questions classified as negative in tone. INC follows at 16%, slightly above BJP at 13%. This is consistent with the incumbency gradient: opposition parties have more reason to frame questions as government failures. But the Left’s high rate is notable even adjusting for their perpetual opposition status across all three Lok Sabhas.



Mean VADER compound score by party with 95% confidence bands, faceted by government vs opposition status.



Do adversarialism levels change over time?



Adversarialism by party across 16th–18th Lok Sabhas. The 18th LS (2024–) is the most recent and shows the sharpest partisan divergence yet.


The temporal pattern shows a widening gap. Opposition parties (particularly INC and the Left) become more adversarial across Lok Sabhas, while BJP’s tone stays relatively stable. Whether this reflects deteriorating political relations, a more combative opposition strategy, or the changing nature of issues in Parliament is not identified here.


Which ministries attract the most critical questioning?



Ministry-level adversarialism rate. Fill colour = mean compound score. Ministries with 50+ starred questions shown.


Home Affairs and Finance attract the most adversarial questioning. That tracks the ministry-targeting analysis above: these are the ministries INC and the Left concentrate on, and they concentrate there because the questions they ask about security and fiscal policy are inherently more confrontational.


Which topics attract adversarial framing?



BERTopic topics by mean compound score (x) and adversarialism rate (y). Topics toward the bottom-left attract the most critical questioning.


The topic-level view maps cleanly onto the subject matter. Topics about judicial matters, security incidents, and minority welfare attract the most adversarial framing. Topics about infrastructure schemes, tourism, and sports attract almost none.






Note



VADER caveat. VADER was calibrated on social media and news text, not formal parliamentary English. The parliamentary register inflates positive scores because declarative, neutral language reads as “not negative.” Absolute score levels are not meaningful; the within-sample variation across parties and ministries is.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Geographic Focus: Where Parties Look
Every question comes with text that mentions specific places. spaCy’s NER pipeline (en_core_web_sm) extracts the states named in each question. This lets us ask: do regional parties really concentrate their parliamentary activity on their home states? And how does BJP’s geographic spread as a national governing party compare?
The answer is stark.



Share of each party’s matched starred questions that name each state. Blank = not mentioned. Labels shown for shares ≥ 0.5%.


Regional parties are entirely concentrated. Left mentions only Kerala. TDP mentions only Telangana and Andhra Pradesh. DMK’s state mentions go almost entirely to Tamil Nadu-adjacent issues. JDU’s geographic focus on Bihar is the highest HHI among parties with substantial question volume.
BJP is the most diffuse. As the national governing party, its MPs raise questions touching 14 different states. No single state accounts for more than 15% of its geographic mentions.

Geographic concentration index



Herfindahl index of state mentions by party. JDU and Left are the most geographically concentrated; BJP is the most diffuse.

HHI = 1 means all state mentions go to one state; HHI near 0 means uniform spread. INC’s Kerala concentration reflects the large number of Kerala MPs in the matched sample.


Who gets named?



Top individuals named per party in starred questions (spaCy PERSON entities). Parliamentary boilerplate titles excluded.


Person naming follows a pattern: government parties name bureaucrats and scheme beneficiaries, opposition parties name politicians they are holding accountable. The specific individuals vary but the structural difference is consistent.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Co-Questioning Networks
Every starred question lists co-signatories alongside the primary MP. About 39% of questions have two or more MPs attached, a structural feature of Indian parliamentary practice where parties coordinate to ensure ministerial coverage. This creates a natural network: an edge between two MPs every time they co-sign a question.
The network has 324 MPs (matched nodes), 739 co-signing pairs, and a graph density of 0.014. The overwhelming pattern is within-party. MPs co-sign almost exclusively with their own party.



Full MP co-questioning network. Node colour = party. Edge colour: grey = within-party, orange = cross-party. Node size = total co-signed questions (weighted degree). Labels on top 3% by betweenness.



How partisan is it?
Louvain community detection finds 15 communities with modularity 0.656, which is very high. A random network with the same degree sequence would have modularity near 0; 0.656 means co-questioning is strongly partitioned by party identity. BJP dominates 13 of the 15 communities. Only 2 communities are >80% a single party (pure); the remaining 13 are mixed but BJP-majority, reflecting BJP’s sheer size advantage.


Cross-party coordination: who bridges party lines?
Filtering to cross-party edges only reveals the structure of coalition politics rather than party discipline.



Cross-party co-questioning edges only. Each edge connects MPs from different parties. Edge width = number of questions co-signed together. Labels = MPs with 3+ cross-party partners.


The top cross-party pairs are almost all NDA coalition partners: BJP-JDU, BJP-Shiv Sena, BJP-Regional (BJD/TDP bloc). These are parties that governed together, allocated portfolio questions jointly, and coordinated legislative agendas. The clearest case: C.N. Annadurai (DMK) co-signed 4 questions with a Regional party MP. DMK was in the INDIA opposition alliance and coordinated with non-BJP regional parties.


Cross-party rate has fallen over time



Cross-party co-questioning rate by Lok Sabha. 16th LS (2014–2019) had 44% cross-party pairs; 17th LS (2019–2024) dropped to 34%.


Cross-party co-signing fell from 44% in the 16th LS to 34% in the 17th LS. One interpretation: as BJP’s NDA coalition became more institutionalised, formal coordination replaced the informal cross-signing that characterised the coalition’s early years. Another: partisan polarisation hardened, making cross-party coordination politically costlier.


Who are the bridge MPs?



Top 25 MPs by betweenness centrality. High betweenness = MP lies on the shortest path between otherwise disconnected parts of the network.


Betweenness centrality identifies MPs who bridge otherwise disconnected clusters. These are not necessarily the most prolific co-signers; they are the connectors whose absence would most fragment the network. The top connectors are predominantly BJP MPs from swing states (Bihar, Jharkhand, Rajasthan) who co-sign across regional and national party groupings. A JDU MP (Bihar-NDA) bridging the BJP cluster to the regional bloc is a recurring pattern.


Party × party co-questioning matrix



Party co-questioning matrix. Diagonal = within-party co-signing. Off-diagonal = cross-party pairs. Cell values = total co-signed questions.


The diagonal dominates. Within-party co-signing is an order of magnitude larger than cross-party. Off-diagonal entries tell the coalition story: BJP-JDU and BJP-Shiv Sena are the strongest cross-party pairs. INC has almost no cross-party co-signing, consistent with its role as a standalone opposition bloc in all three Lok Sabhas.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Manifestos vs Parliament
Election manifestos are the public contract between a party and its voters. But parliament is where that contract is tested. Two questions: do parties actually raise in parliament what they promised in their manifestos? And does the opposition use parliament to hold the ruling party to its own commitments?
Sixteen manifestos are compared: BJP and INC across three elections (2014, 2019, 2024), plus AIADMK, NCP, AITC, CPI(M), and DMK where data permits. For each party × Lok Sabha combination, we compute the TF-IDF cosine similarity between manifesto word frequencies and the starred question word frequencies of that party’s MPs in the subsequent parliament. Higher cosine means more manifesto language actually surfaces in questions.
All MP names, constituency names, and parliamentary boilerplate are stripped before the comparison to ensure we’re measuring policy vocabulary, not procedural noise.

How aligned are parties with their own manifestos?



TF-IDF cosine similarity between each party’s election manifesto and its MPs’ subsequent starred questions in the corresponding Lok Sabha. Both parties sustain meaningful but imperfect alignment. BJP scores slightly higher than INC throughout. INC dips in the 17th LS after its second consecutive election loss.








NoteWhat the numbers mean



Both parties land in the 0.18–0.24 cosine range, meaningfully above zero (random text would score near zero) but well below 1.0 (identical vocabulary). Manifestos and questions are written in different registers for different audiences, so perfect alignment is neither expected nor desirable. The interesting signal is the variation.
BJP (0.239 → 0.225 → 0.223): Highest in the 16th LS, where the 2014 election carried the biggest mandate and the freshest manifesto agenda. The gradual drift across three terms suggests that as governance proceeds, the parliamentary agenda increasingly reflects emerging crises and administrative demands rather than original manifesto promises.
INC (0.207 → 0.183 → 0.202): The 17th LS (2019–24) shows INC’s lowest alignment. After a second consecutive landslide defeat, INC entered the 17th Lok Sabha organisationally weakened. Their questions in this period are more reactive, responding to events, than manifesto-driven. The partial recovery in the 18th LS as the INDIA alliance consolidates is visible.
The ruling party effect: BJP consistently outscores INC by ~3–5 percentage points. A ruling party directly shapes policy implementation and can use its own MPs’ questions to highlight scheme performance and progress, tightening the link between manifesto promises and parliamentary activity. The opposition’s questions are partly dictated by whatever crises arise, pulling them off-manifesto.




What did BJP promise but not question?
The gap between a party’s manifesto vocabulary and its parliamentary questions reveals which promises remain on paper and which get translated into legislative scrutiny.



Orange bars = words prominent in BJP manifestos but relatively absent from BJP MPs’ starred questions. Navy = topics BJP MPs questioned heavily that barely appear in their manifestos. The asymmetry reveals two different registers of political language.








NoteTwo registers of political language



The gap is not a failure of follow-through. It reflects a structural difference between campaign rhetoric and parliamentary procedure.
Promised but not questioned (orange): Words like global, expand, nation, governance, citizens, expanding, ensure dominate the manifesto-only side. These are aspiration words that describe a direction of travel rather than a specific policy instrument. They are effective on the campaign trail but have no natural home in a starred question, which must be concrete enough to demand a factual ministerial reply.
Questioned beyond the manifesto (navy): Scheme, funds, farmers, pradesh, reasons, department, projects are almost invisible in the manifesto but dominate BJP MPs’ actual questions. These are the words of governance administration: asking how many funds have been released, why a project is delayed, what the scheme eligibility criteria are. The manifesto doesn’t say “scheme-wise fund release status”; it says “we will transform India.” Parliament inverts this: questions are granular, evidence-demanding, and state-specific.
This is a general finding that would apply to any party: manifestos operate in the language of vision, parliament operates in the language of accountability.




Does the opposition hold BJP to its own promises?
If parliament functions as an accountability mechanism, INC MPs should be picking up BJP’s manifesto vocabulary and asking whether the commitments have been met. The figure below shows which BJP manifesto words INC adopted and which they left alone.



Left panel: BJP manifesto words that INC MPs actively used in starred questions. These are the promises INC chose to scrutinise. Right panel: BJP manifesto words INC MPs largely did not pick up.








NoteINC’s accountability strategy: broad but shallow



INC MPs pick up almost every major BJP manifesto word (development, education, infrastructure, health, water, technology, employment, sector, quality) but at volumes typically 30–60% lower than BJP MPs ask about the same topics. This is consistent with a broad monitoring strategy: INC tracks the full manifesto agenda rather than concentrating on a small set of high-priority promises.
The notable exception is nation, a word BJP uses 112 times across its manifestos but which INC MPs use in zero starred questions. This is not accidental. Nation (rashtra) as a political concept has been rhetorically claimed by BJP as its own terrain. INC MPs avoid the word in parliamentary questions, which are factual rather than ideological, where it would be impossible to contest BJP’s ownership of the concept.
The words INC does not pick up from BJP manifestos tend to be BJP-specific branding terms (yojana names, scheme-specific jargon) rather than substantive policy areas. INC monitors the policy; it largely ignores the packaging.




Did BJP MPs keep questioning their 2014 agenda across three terms?
The 2014 manifesto was BJP’s most consequential in a generation, promising infrastructure, manufacturing, digital India, and good governance after a decade of UPA rule. How much of that original agenda survived in BJP MPs’ parliamentary questions across three Lok Sabhas?



Each line tracks one of BJP’s top 2014 manifesto keywords in BJP MPs’ starred questions across the 16th, 17th, and 18th Lok Sabha. A rising line = gaining traction; a falling line = fading parliamentary attention.








NoteThe 2014 agenda in parliament: what stuck and what faded



The trajectory reveals three types of manifesto words:
Durable anchors: words like development, farmers, health, water, rural that remain consistently prominent across all three Lok Sabhas. These reflect India’s structural governance challenges that no government can defer.
Rising items: topics that gain prominence after 2014, suggesting that initial policy initiatives created new parliamentary activity around them. Infrastructure and technology show this pattern.
Fading promises: words that were prominent in BJP’s 2014 manifesto but decline in question frequency across terms. These may represent promises that were either delivered (reducing the need to question), quietly dropped, or superseded by newer priorities in the 2019 and 2024 manifestos.
The overall picture is one of gradual drift: BJP’s questions in the 18th LS resemble their 2014 manifesto significantly less than their 16th LS questions did, consistent with the alignment score decline (0.239 → 0.223).




Manifesto vocabulary vs question vocabulary: BJP and INC side by side
A direct comparison of the top words in each party’s manifesto and questions reveals the translation loss between the two documents.



Top 20 most distinctive words per party per source. Green bars appear in both the manifesto top-20 AND the questions top-20, indicating genuine overlap. Grey bars appear in only one. The proportion of green reveals how much manifesto language actually bleeds into parliament.








NoteWhat both parties share and where they diverge



Shared manifesto territory: Both BJP and INC manifestos heavily feature development, national, education, women, infrastructure, security, policy, services, health, water. The overlap is striking: Indian political parties at the national level compete on remarkably similar governance agendas.
BJP manifesto distinctives: Manufacturing, mission, technology, governance, global, tribal. BJP’s economic nationalism frame and its specific scheme branding (yojana, mission) are more prominent than in INC’s manifesto.
INC manifesto distinctives: Promises, rights, institutions, scheduled, farmers, growth. INC’s language centres more on entitlements, institutional protection, and explicitly naming vulnerable groups. The word promises itself appears 211 times in INC manifestos across three elections, a self-referential acknowledgment of the accountability contract.
The questions register: Both parties’ questions converge on scheme, funds, farmers, states, projects, welfare, water, the operational vocabulary of parliamentary oversight. The ideological differences of the manifesto largely disappear in the question hall.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Opposition Discipline Index
A disciplined parliamentary party speaks with one voice: its MPs ask about the same topics using similar vocabulary. An undisciplined one has MPs pulling in different directions, each following their own constituency agenda. This section quantifies that divergence.
Method: For each MP with at least 5 starred questions, we build a TF-IDF vector over their question vocabulary. The discipline score for a party is the average cosine similarity between each MP’s vector and the party’s centroid (pooled) vector. A score near 1 means every MP sounds the same; a score near 0 means every MP is asking about different things.

How coordinated is each party?



Average cosine similarity of each MP’s question vocabulary to their party’s centroid. Higher = MPs converge on shared vocabulary = more coordinated questioning. Numbers in parentheses show the total MP count included.








NoteThe counterintuitive result: BJP is less disciplined than INC



BJP scores 0.094 in the 16th LS vs INC’s 0.181: INC is notably more coordinated despite being in opposition. This flips the conventional narrative. The explanation is structural:
Why ruling parties are less disciplined: BJP sent 272–303 MPs to each Lok Sabha. With that many MPs spread across hundreds of constituencies, the party cannot enforce a single questioning agenda. Each MP has their own local infrastructure project, state scheme, or sectoral concern. The result is a wide vocabulary of questions that reduces the average similarity to any centroid.
Why smaller opposition parties are more disciplined: INC (44–52 eligible MPs in our data) has fewer MPs, and each MP carries a higher proportion of the party’s total questioning load. Smaller parties like Left and TDP score highest precisely because they have 5–15 MPs all focused on a narrow issue set (labour rights, Andhra Pradesh concerns).
Regional parties: Left, BSP, TDP, JDU all score above 0.3, well above both national parties. These are single-issue or single-region parties where thematic focus is by design.




Discipline across three Lok Sabhas



Discipline score by party across the 16th, 17th and 18th Lok Sabha. Error bars show standard error.








Note



BJP’s discipline rises from 0.094 in the 16th LS to 0.129 in the 18th. The 18th Lok Sabha returned a smaller BJP cohort (240 seats vs 303 in the 17th), forcing the party to deploy fewer MPs on a similar range of questions. A smaller caucus is mechanically more coordinated.
INC also rises, from 0.181 in the 16th to 0.149 in the 18th, consistent with the INDIA alliance giving INC a clearer opposition identity and reducing the reactive, event-driven questioning that depressed the 17th LS score.




Which BJP and INC MPs diverge most from their party?



Each dot is one MP in the 16th, 17th or 18th Lok Sabha. Y-axis = cosine similarity to party centroid. MPs at the bottom have question vocabularies most different from their party’s average, driven by local/personal agendas. MPs at the top are the party’s most “on-message” questioners.




BJP vs INC discipline over time



Trend line of BJP and INC discipline scores across three Lok Sabhas. Note the convergence in the 18th LS as BJP’s seat count fell and INC’s alliance coordination improved.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Religion and Parliamentary Questions
Indian parliamentary democracy has no formal religious caucus system. But if identity shapes agenda, religious minority MPs might systematically direct questions toward ministries that serve their communities. Name-pattern inference identifies Muslim, Sikh, and Christian MPs and tests whether their questioning behaviour differs from the majority.
Method: Islamic first names (Mohammad, Abdul, Syed, Ghulam, etc.) and surnames (Ansari, Siddiqui, Qureshi, etc.) flag Muslim MPs. Punjabi first names combined with Singh/Kaur flag Sikh MPs. South Indian Christian surnames (Varghese, Mathew, Thomas, George, Sebastian, etc.) flag Christian MPs. All others are pooled as Other/Hindu. This is a lower bound: Muslim MPs without Arabic-origin names are not captured.






Note



Coverage: 216 starred questions (1.8% of corpus) are from Muslim-identified MPs. 114 from Sikh-identified MPs, 154 from Christian-identified MPs. These are lower bounds: Muslim MPs represent roughly 5-6% of Parliament seats, so the identification rate of 1.3% reflects naming pattern limits, not the actual share.



Do Muslim MPs target different ministries?



Percentage point difference in ministry share: Muslim MPs minus non-Muslim MPs. Positive = Muslim MPs disproportionately question this ministry. The Minority Affairs result is the clearest signal.








NoteMinority Affairs is the strongest signal, but not the whole story



Minority Affairs is the ministry Muslim MPs disproportionately target, which is the expected finding: the ministry exists precisely to serve religious minorities and Muslim MPs use parliament to scrutinise it.
What is more interesting are the secondary signals. Muslim MPs also over-index on ministries covering urban affairs, housing, labour, and education, reflecting the socioeconomic concentration of India’s Muslim population in urban centres with specific infrastructure and employment deficits.
The ministry Muslim MPs question less than others reveals the political constraints on minority MPs in the current parliament. Topics like Defence and Home Affairs, which carry strong majoritarian framing, are under-represented in Muslim MPs’ question portfolios relative to their share of parliament. This is consistent with minority MPs avoiding terrain coded as politically hostile.




Which parties send Muslim MPs to the question floor?



Share of each party’s starred questions where the primary MP is identified as Muslim. Parties are sorted by Muslim MP questioning share.








Note



The distribution of Muslim MP questions across parties reflects both seat allocation (which parties field Muslim candidates) and question activity (how actively those MPs use starred questions). Regional parties from states with large Muslim populations (SP, RJD, AIMIM) show high shares. INC’s Muslim MP share reflects its historical Muslim voter base and the candidates it fields. BJP’s near-zero share is consistent with its candidate selection pattern across three Lok Sabhas.




What policy vocabulary do Muslim MPs use more?



Log2 ratio of word frequencies: Muslim MPs vs all other MPs. Filtered to words appearing at least 10 times total. Positive = Muslim MPs use this word proportionally more in their questions.




Do religious minorities disproportionately question the Minority Affairs Ministry?



Percentage of each religion group’s starred questions directed at the Minority Affairs Ministry.








Note



The Minority Affairs questioning rate by religion confirms the identity-representation mechanism: Muslim MPs question Minority Affairs at the highest rate, followed by Christian MPs. Sikh MPs show a relatively lower rate, consistent with Sikh political identity being more strongly channelled through Punjab-state issues (agriculture, water, Chandigarh) than through the central Minority Affairs Ministry. The Other/Hindu baseline is near zero, as expected: Minority Affairs has no natural constituency among majority-community MPs.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
section: Electoral Incentives: Do Margins Shape Questions?
text:
Electoral Incentives: Do Margins Shape Questions?
The electoral connection in legislatures is one of the central mechanisms in political economy: legislators who fear losing their seat should work harder to signal effort to constituents. Starred questions are one of the few visible, public effort signals available to Indian MPs. The analysis links ECI election results (2014 and 2019) to question behavior to test whether the margin of victory shapes how MPs use the instrument.
Method: Margin of victory is computed as (winner votes - runner-up votes) / total votes cast, using the Bhavnani national election dataset (2014) and the ECI 2019 constituency-level results. MPs are classified as Marginal (margin below 10%), Competitive (10-25%), or Safe (above 25%). Merged to Lok Sabha question data by normalized constituency name.



Marginal seat MPs ask fewer starred questions on average. Each point represents one margin-of-victory decile; the dashed line is the OLS fit. The upward slope confirms that electoral security, not insecurity, is associated with higher question activity.








Note



The electoral security hypothesis predicts the opposite of what most accountability theories expect. Marginal seat MPs (average 13.8 questions in the 16th LS, 11.2 in the 17th) ask fewer starred questions than competitive (13.5 in the 16th) or safe seat MPs. This is not because safe seat MPs are more motivated: it reflects selection. Safe seats are typically won by experienced, senior politicians who have built up a legislative practice over multiple terms. Marginal seats often feature first-term MPs still learning the institution. The margin effect is partly a tenure effect.



What topics do marginal vs safe seat MPs focus on?



Top words by log2 ratio: safe seat MPs vs marginal seat MPs. Positive = safe seat MPs use this word disproportionately. Negative = marginal seat MPs use it more.








Note



The vocabulary divergence is more revealing than the volume difference. Safe seat MPs use more abstract, institutional vocabulary (finance, policy, regulation, national schemes). Marginal seat MPs show higher rates of words tied to local delivery and specific central schemes: terms connected to road, water, rural, employment, and district-level programs. This is consistent with constituent service signaling under electoral pressure: MPs whose seats are at risk shift toward visible, local deliverables rather than broad policy engagement.




Question intensity across the Lok Sabha cycle



Normalised question volume by session, 16th Lok Sabha (2014-2019). Shaded area marks the final three sessions before dissolution.


All three MP groups increase activity in pre-dissolution sessions, but the spike is sharpest for marginal seat MPs, consistent with electoral signaling intensifying as the election approaches.


Ministry targeting by seat safety



Percentage point difference in ministry share: safe seat MPs minus marginal seat MPs. Positive = safe MPs over-index; negative = marginal MPs over-index.


Safe seat MPs over-index on Finance, External Affairs, and Home Affairs: ministries with national policy scope. Marginal seat MPs over-index on Rural Development, Agriculture, and Housing: ministries whose outputs are visible and attributable at the constituency level. The pattern is consistent with the distributive politics literature on targeted spending and credit-claiming under electoral competition.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Ideal Points from Question Vocabulary
A standard approach to measuring political positions in legislatures is ideal point estimation: project legislators into a low-dimensional space where proximity means similarity in behavior. NOMINATE does this for US roll-call votes; the same logic applies to question vocabulary using truncated SVD. Each MP is represented by their TF-IDF word vector; truncated SVD extracts the two dimensions that explain the most cross-MP variation.
Method: Build a 860-MP by 5,251-word TF-IDF matrix (MPs as documents, words as features). Column-center to remove average term frequency (equivalent to mean-centering before PCA). Run truncated SVD via irlba; extract 2 components. The left singular vectors scaled by singular values give MP coordinates; right singular vectors give word loadings.



2D ideal point scatter for 860 Indian MPs across the 16th-18th Lok Sabha. Each point is one MP; diamonds are party centroids.








Note



The central finding is what does not appear: Indian MPs do not sort into a clear left-right spectrum in question vocabulary. BJP, INC, Left, and Shiv Sena centroids all cluster near the origin. TDP is the clearest outlier, displaced strongly on Dimension 2. This is the opposite of what NOMINATE finds for US Congress, where a single ideological axis explains most roll-call variation.
The explanation is structural: starred questions are not party-whipped. Unlike roll-call votes, MPs face no party pressure on which topics to raise. The result is that question vocabulary variation is dominated by constituency-level agendas (which state the MP represents, which industries their constituents work in, which central schemes matter locally) rather than by party ideology. The ideal point space recovers geographic and sectoral cleavages, not ideological ones.



Dimension 1: the primary axis of variation



Distribution of MP ideal points along Dimension 1, by party. Sorted by median. Overlap between parties is high.


Dimension 1 explains the most variance but does not track party affiliation. The distributions for BJP, INC, Left, and Regional parties largely overlap. This is the key finding: the primary source of variation in what Indian MPs ask about is not which party they belong to, but who their constituents are.


Dimension 2: regional parties diverge



Distribution of MP ideal points along Dimension 2, by party.


Dimension 2 shows more party-level structure. TDP is strongly separated (negative), consistent with TDP MPs focusing heavily on Andhra Pradesh-specific issues (Polavaram dam, Amaravati, bifurcation consequences). The Left occupies a distinct positive position, reflecting their labour and welfare vocabulary. National parties (BJP, INC) cluster at the centre, consistent with their need to cover the full range of national policy.


What drives the dimensions? Word loadings



Word loadings in the 2D SVD space. Words in each quadrant define what that direction measures. Words far from origin have strong loading on both dimensions.


The word loadings reveal the substantive content of each dimension. Words with high Dimension 2 loading tend to be state-specific or sector-specific terms that regional parties monopolise. Words near the origin are universally used parliamentary vocabulary that provides no discrimination. The loading plot confirms that Dimension 2 is the more politically interpretable axis.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
section: Rajya Sabha: Testing the Constituency Hypothesis
text:
Rajya Sabha: Testing the Constituency Hypothesis
The J1 ideal points analysis found no party clustering in Lok Sabha question vocabulary. The explanation was structural: starred questions are constituency-service instruments, so vocabulary variation is driven by where an MP’s constituents live, not which party they belong to.
Rajya Sabha members provide a direct test. They represent states, not individual constituencies. If constituency variation is the main reason Lok Sabha parties don’t cluster, Rajya Sabha members should show more party-based vocabulary structure. The identical SVD pipeline runs on 4907 Rajya Sabha starred questions (2014-2025) from 264 matched members, covering roughly 97% of the starred RS question archive.

The comparison



Normalised party centroids on the primary SVD dimension, Lok Sabha vs Rajya Sabha. Lines connect the same party across houses. Wider spread between parties = more party-based vocabulary differentiation.








NoteThe result: constituency variation explains part of the story, not all of it



Lok Sabha BJP-INC Dim1 spread (normalised): 0.175
Rajya Sabha BJP-INC Dim1 spread (normalised): 1.373
Removing individual constituency variation does produce more party separation. The BJP centroid (0.00552) and INC centroid (-0.00206) are further apart in Rajya Sabha than their Lok Sabha equivalents (-0.00112 vs -9.3^{-4}). The normalised BJP-INC spread is roughly 8 times larger.
Within-party variance still dominates, though. The between-party variance is about 2% of the within-party variance (ratio = 0.018). Even in Rajya Sabha, individual members vary enormously around their party’s centroid. For comparison, US Congress NOMINATE produces between/within ratios above 10. Indian parliamentary questions, even in the upper house, do not sort cleanly by party.
The constituency effect is real and accounts for a large share of the J1 finding. But something else is also at work: party coordination over starred questions is weak in both houses. MPs across both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha follow their own judgment on what to ask, shaped by background, state, seniority, and committee assignments rather than a party whip.




Rajya Sabha ideal points



2D ideal point scatter for 264 Rajya Sabha members. BJP (68 members) and INC (69 members) centroids are now visibly separated, unlike in Lok Sabha where they are nearly coincident.




Distribution along Dimension 1



Party distributions along the primary SVD dimension (Rajya Sabha). Parties show more separation than in the Lok Sabha equivalent, but distributions still overlap substantially.








NoteWhat Dimension 1 captures in Rajya Sabha



In Lok Sabha (J1), Dimension 1 captured a constituency-type axis: agrarian vs urban questions. In Rajya Sabha, where that constituency variation is removed, Dimension 1 begins to pick up party-line vocabulary differences – BJP members clustering toward infrastructure and national scheme terminology, INC members clustering toward accountability and governance language. The separation is modest but directionally consistent with party ideology.
Left parties and BJD (Biju Janata Dal, Odisha) sit further along the dimension, consistent with their narrower, more disciplined question agendas. BJD’s position reflects Odisha-specific vocabulary (Mahanadi water dispute, tribal affairs, POSCO project) that makes it distinct from both national parties.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
Gender and Representation
Women MPs are identified through parliamentary honorifics (Shrimati, Smt, Kumari, Ms, Mrs) in the official question records. SC/ST status is identified from the (SC) and (ST) reservation tags attached to constituency names in the MP lookup, which is the cleanest proxy available without external affidavit data.






Note



Coverage: 292 starred questions (2.5% of the corpus) are from women MPs identifiable by honorific. SC reserved-seat MPs account for 13.8% of questions; ST reserved-seat MPs for 7.2%. The gender figure is a lower bound; women MPs who appear without honorific in the records are not captured.



Do women MPs target different ministries?



Women MPs’ questions are disproportionately directed at Women & Child Development, Health, and Education. Men MPs concentrate more on infrastructure-adjacent ministries. The gap confirms a constituency-of-identity effect alongside geographic constituency effects.




Are women MPs more or less adversarial?



VADER compound score distributions by gender. A rightward shift = more neutral framing.




What language is distinctive to women MPs?



Log-ratio of word frequencies (women vs men MPs), filtered to words appearing at least 10 times in the corpus. Positive = women use this word proportionally more. The vocabulary reveals a welfare and rights frame distinct from the infrastructure and sectoral frame of male MPs.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
text:
SC/ST Representation
Reserved constituencies are constitutionally mandated to return SC or ST MPs. The question is whether these MPs use their parliamentary voice differently: do they redirect questions toward ministries that directly serve their communities?

Ministry targeting: SC and ST vs general seats



Top panel: SC reserved-seat MPs’ ministry focus relative to general-seat MPs. Bottom panel: ST reserved-seat MPs. The ST result is unambiguous: Tribal Affairs is dramatically over-represented, as expected. The SC result shows Social Justice and Labour elevated, but also some unexpected concentrations.


The ST pattern is the cleaner finding: MPs from tribal reserved constituencies disproportionately direct questions at Tribal Affairs, Rural Development, and Forests, the ministries most directly affecting their electorate. The SC pattern is more diffuse, reflecting the broader geographic spread of SC constituencies across India’s economic landscape.


SC/ST question volume by party



Number of starred questions from SC and ST reserved-seat MPs, broken down by party. BJP has the largest absolute volume given its size, but the share figures tell a different story about which parties activate their SC/ST MPs most.

title: What Does India’s Parliament Actually Talk About?
section: What the Results Do Not Tell You
text:
What the Results Do Not Tell You

The incumbency confound
Parliamentary questions are not a pure record of ideology. They are also a record of political position. A party in government asks: is the highway project on schedule? Is fertiliser subsidy reaching farmers? A party in opposition asks: why is the auditor’s report suppressed? Why are minorities being targeted? These are structurally different questions, and the difference is positional, not necessarily ideological.
The figure below documents this directly. Across the 205 party-session documents, NDA government parties systematically emphasise different topics than opposition parties, independent of any ideological interpretation.



Government vs. opposition topic profiles across 15 LDA topics. Positive bars indicate topics asked about more by NDA government parties; negative bars indicate topics asked about more by opposition parties.




Why this cannot be fixed with the available data
The natural response is to partial out incumbency status and see what remains. The problem is that this only works if some parties switch sides across the sample period, giving within-party variation to identify the incumbency effect separately from ideology. In this dataset, BJP is in government for every Lok Sabha observed, and INC is in opposition for every Lok Sabha observed. So incumbency status is perfectly collinear with party identity for the two parties the comparison hinges on. Regressing out in_gov does not remove the incumbency effect. It removes the BJP-INC contrast itself, which is circular.
The only genuine switchers are TDP (NDA in 16th LS, opposition in 17th, NDA again in 18th) and JDU (opposition in 16th, NDA in 17th and 18th). TDP won only 3 Lok Sabha seats in 2019, so its 17th LS corpus is too sparse. That leaves JDU as the primary source of within-party variation, not enough to credibly identify the incumbency effect across 13 parties.
The STM with incumbency added as a covariate (~ bjp + inc + left + lok_num + session + in_gov) does not solve this either. When party indicators and in_gov are jointly included in the prevalence formula, the model faces the same multicollinearity. The in_gov coefficient is identified mainly from the three switchers.



STM party effects (BJP, INC, Left) and the incumbency effect, estimated jointly. The incumbency coefficient is identified primarily from TDP and JDU switching; BJP and INC coefficients remain confounded with their respective incumbency status.




What follows from this
The main findings should be read as descriptive text differences, not identified ideological positions. The claim that BJP MPs and Left MPs write differently is well-supported. They clearly do. Whether that difference reflects ideology, government responsibilities, or both is not separable with this research design.
Proper identification would require an instrument for coalition entry (something that predicts which parties join NDA but is uncorrelated with their vocabulary independent of incumbency) or a regression discontinuity around close elections that determine coalition composition. Neither is feasible here.
The BJP trajectory finding (Hindutva score rising monotonically across three Lok Sabhas) faces the additional problem of having only three data points, and the political context changed sharply between them: the Ayodhya verdict, CAA, and Article 370 revocation all fall between the 16th and 17th LS. The text shift is real; whether it is ideological deepening or a contextual response is not identified.
